g.data-narration: The Programmer's Interface is the bridge between application software and the operating system, enabling requests for services like file operations and process management.
g.text: The mechanisms through which application software interacts with the OS and hardware resources.
It provides services like file operations, process management, memory allocation, and IPC.
Two primary interfaces: Command Language Interface (CLI) and API

g.data-narration: Understanding this interface is essential as it forms the foundation of system programming and software development in any programming language.
g.text: • Foundation for system programming and software development
• Write programs that interact with OS without understanding kernel internals
• Essential for Operating Systems, System Programming, and UNIX Programming courses

g.data-narration: System calls are the fundamental interface between user programs and the OS kernel, requesting services like reading files or creating processes.
g.text: • Request from user program to OS kernel for privileged operations
• Only way to transition from user mode (ring 3) to kernel mode (ring 0)
• Each call has a unique number; kernel maintains a system call table
• After kernel completes operation, control returns with results or error codes

g.data-narration: System calls are categorized into six main types: Process Control, File Management, Device Management, Information Maintenance, Communication, and Protection.
g.text: Process: fork(), exec(), wait(), exit(), kill()
File: open(), close(), read(), write(), lseek(), unlink()

g.data-narration: Modern OS use protection rings - user mode cannot access hardware directly while kernel mode has full access to all system resources.
g.text: Requests kernel services via system calls

g.data-narration: An API is a higher-level interface that may wrap multiple system calls, while a system call specifically requests kernel services with mode switching.
Library functions like printf() execute in user mode, while system calls involve kernel mode transition with significant overhead.
g.text: Execute in user mode • May or may not make system calls
Transition to kernel mode • Context switch overhead
Note: printf() formats in user space, calls write() syscall only when outputting. malloc() uses heap in user space.

g.data-narration: This example shows file operations using system calls: open() creates a file descriptor, read() reads data, and close() releases resources.
g.text: Example: File Read Using System Calls
Standard file descriptors: stdin=0, stdout=1, stderr=2. open() returns 3, 4, 5...

g.data-narration: fork() creates a new process by duplicating the current process. It returns 0 in child and child's PID in parent.
g.text: Duplicates current process → Parent gets child's PID

g.data-narration: Pipes provide unidirectional IPC: pipe() creates read[0] and write[1] ends, parent writes and child reads.
g.text: After fork(): parent closes pipefd[0], writes to pipefd[1]; child closes pipefd[1], reads from pipefd[0]

g.data-narration: Key points: System calls are the only kernel request method with six categories, requiring mode switches. APIs provide higher-level abstraction.
g.text: ✓ System calls are the ONLY way user programs request kernel services
✓ Six categories: Process control, File mgmt, Device mgmt, Info maint, Communication, Protection
✓ Library functions may not make system calls; system calls always involve kernel

g.data-narration: Remember: the six system call categories, user vs kernel mode differences, and why system calls are necessary for protection and resource management.
g.text: Close file descriptors to prevent leaks
